victim / password123

NimbusCloud
ResourcesAPIsBillingSupport
—

Developer Console

Manage your cloud resources and API access

User Lookup Fetches /v1/users/info/:id

Look up info for any user in your organization:

// Output appears here
Your Access Token /v1/token (sensitive, requires auth)

Fetch your current API access token. This endpoint returns Cache-Control: no-store.

// Your token appears here
🎯 Attacker View No auth required

This panel simulates what an unauthenticated attacker sees when they fetch from the CDN. If they know a cached sensitive URL, they get its content — no session needed.

// Attacker output
📦 Simulated CDN Cache

Empty cache

ℹ️ CDN Behavior

• URLs ending in .css .js .png .jpg .gif .svg are cached for 24h

• Cache key is the URL path only (headers ignored)

• Cached responses are served before auth check

• Cache-Control response header is overridden for static extensions

Hint: CSPT + Cache Deception → ATO

This is a two-bug chain. The sensitive /v1/token endpoint returns your auth token but is not cacheable. The user lookup endpoint has a path traversal. Combine them: force the token response to be cached at a path the attacker can fetch. Based on zere's article.
1
Understand the CDN›
The CDN sits in front of the origin. It caches any URL ending in .css .js .png .jpg .gif .svg and serves cached responses to ANYONE (no auth check). The cache key is just the URL path — headers are ignored.
2
The sensitive endpoint›
/cache-api/v1/token returns your access token. It requires session auth. Normally it's not cached (no static extension). But what if we could trick the browser into sending the request to a path ending in .css?
3
The CSPT in user lookup›
The user lookup code does: 
var url = "/cache-api/v1/users/info/" + userId;
fetch(url, {credentials: "include"});
If userId contains ../, the browser normalizes the URL before sending. You control the final path.
4
Craft the traversal›
Starting from /cache-api/v1/users/info/, you need two ../ to get back to /cache-api/v1/, then token.css:
../../token.css
5
The chain›
(a) Victim enters ../../token.css as user ID (or clicks a crafted URL)
(b) Browser normalizes → fetch /cache-api/v1/token.css with their session cookie
(c) Server returns the token (the /v1/token* handler matches)
(d) CDN sees .css extension, caches the response
(e) Attacker fetches /cache-api/v1/token.css with no auth — gets cached token

Step 1 (as victim): In the User Lookup field, enter:

../../token.css

Click "Look Up". Watch the cache panel — a new entry for /cache-api/v1/token.css appears with your token.

Step 2 (as attacker): In the Attacker View, the URL is already /cache-api/v1/token.css. Click "Fetch Without Auth".

The CDN serves the cached response. No session cookie needed. Attacker gets the token → full account takeover.

Why this works: Browser normalizes /cache-api/v1/users/info/../../token.css to /cache-api/v1/token.css BEFORE sending. The request has the victim's cookies. Server's /v1/token* route matches. CDN sees .css and caches. Attacker reads cached data.
